title: Mired In The Labor-Market Malaise
text:
Mired In The Labor-Market Malaise

Ernan Cui

Gavekal Dragonomics | 18 Jun 2026

China’s labor market never truly recovered after the pandemic, and recently released data on jobs and wages in 2025 reveal the extent of the malaise. The share of the workforce engaged in agriculture rose for only the second time in decades, as fewer urban job opportunities pushed more people into farming. Employment in construction is in a slow-motion collapse, and labor demand in services is cooling. The sole bright spot is the manufacturing sector, where hiring and wages are picking up due to robust export growth. But manufacturing alone is not enough to tighten the labor market. More recent jobs data provide little reason to expect an improvement soon, meaning labor-market slack will continue to sap household income and spending growth in the coming months.
